description: Abdominal pain and indigestion (dyspepsia) causing pain, bloating, or discomfort in the upper abdomen. Most cases are not serious and resolve on their own.
key_questions: Where exactly is the pain — upper, lower, left side, or right side of the abdomen?
What did you eat in the last 12-24 hours?
Is the pain sharp and sudden or a dull, constant ache?
Does the pain get worse after eating or on an empty stomach?
Do you feel bloated or gassy?
Have you had any vomiting or diarrhea?
How often do you eat spicy or oily foods?
Are you under significant stress right now?
When was your last bowel movement? Was it normal?
Do you take any painkillers (ibuprofen/aspirin) regularly?
Do you drink alcohol? How often?
Do you experience this pain frequently or is this new?
dos: Drink warm water or herbal tea — peppermint, ginger, or chamomile are excellent for digestion
Eat small, frequent meals instead of 2-3 large ones
Eat bland, easy-to-digest foods: rice, bananas, toast, plain yogurt, boiled potatoes
Apply a warm compress or heating pad to the abdomen for 15-20 minutes
Sit upright for at least 30 minutes after eating — gravity helps digestion
Chew food slowly and thoroughly — aim for 20-30 chews per bite
Try ginger: fresh ginger tea, ginger candy, or grated ginger with honey
Walk slowly for 10-15 minutes after meals — aids digestion
Practice deep breathing during pain episodes — reduces gut muscle spasms
Drink ajwain (carom seed) water — boil 1 tsp in water, strain and sip
Take antacids for acid reflux symptoms (Tums, Gelusil)
Eat probiotic-rich foods: yogurt, buttermilk, kefir — restores gut health
donts: Do NOT eat spicy, fried, or very fatty foods — they worsen indigestion significantly
Do NOT drink carbonated/fizzy drinks — they increase bloating and gas
Do NOT drink alcohol — it directly irritates the stomach lining
Do NOT smoke — nicotine increases stomach acid production
Do NOT eat large meals before bed — increases acid reflux risk dramatically
Do NOT take ibuprofen or aspirin on an empty stomach — causes gastropathy
Do NOT lie down immediately after eating — wait at least 2 hours
Do NOT drink water during meals — drink 30 min before or after meals
Do NOT eat too quickly — rushing meals causes swallowing air and bloating
Do NOT ignore persistent pain lasting more than 48 hours
home_remedies: Ajwain water: Boil 1 tsp carom seeds in a cup of water, add a pinch of black salt
Jeera (cumin) water: Boil 1 tsp cumin seeds in water, strain, drink warm — reduces bloating
Hing (asafoetida) paste: Mix a pinch of hing in warm water, apply around navel area
Fennel seeds: Chew half teaspoon of fennel seeds after meals — natural digestive aid
Buttermilk with roasted cumin: Excellent for acid reflux and bloating
Baking soda water: 1/2 tsp in a glass of water — instant antacid (not for regular use)
dietary_guidelines: Eat: plain rice, bananas, applesauce, toast, boiled potatoes, yogurt, oatmeal, steamed vegetables
Drink: warm water, ginger tea, peppermint tea, buttermilk, coconut water, fennel tea
Avoid: spicy food, fried food, citrus, tomatoes, chocolate, caffeine, alcohol, raw onions, beans (gassy)
warning_signs: Severe or worsening pain that doesn't improve within 24-48 hours
Blood in vomit or stool (black, tarry stool)
Unexplained weight loss
Difficulty swallowing
Pain radiating to chest, arm, or jaw (could be cardiac — call emergency)
Persistent bloating for more than 3 weeks
Fever with abdominal pain
Rigid, board-like abdomen — possible surgical emergency

description: Nausea is the sensation of needing to vomit. Vomiting is the forceful expulsion of stomach contents. Usually a protective mechanism that is self-limiting.
key_questions: When did the nausea/vomiting start?
How many times have you vomited today?
What does the vomit look like — food, bile (yellow/green), or blood?
What was the last thing you ate and when?
Have you been able to keep any fluids down?
Did you travel in a car/bus/boat recently?
Could you be pregnant?
Did you drink alcohol in the last 24 hours?
Are you taking any new medications?
Do you feel dizzy or is the room spinning?
Do you have diarrhea along with the vomiting?
Have others who ate the same food also gotten sick?
dos: Sip clear fluids slowly in small amounts: water, Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS), clear broth
Try ginger in any form — ginger tea, ginger ale, ginger candy, or raw ginger slices
Eat small amounts of bland food only when nausea subsides: crackers, dry toast, plain rice
Rest in a sitting or propped-up position — lying completely flat worsens nausea
Breathe fresh air — step outside or open a window
Apply a cool, damp cloth to the forehead and back of neck
Follow the BRAT diet: Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast — gentle on the stomach
For motion sickness: focus on the horizon or a fixed distant point
Sip ORS or water every 15 minutes after vomiting stops — rehydrate gradually
Try deep, slow breathing — inhale through nose for 4 seconds, exhale through mouth for 6 seconds
Smell fresh lemon or peppermint — both are clinically proven to reduce nausea
Suck on ice chips if unable to drink fluids — provides slow hydration
donts: Do NOT eat large meals — small portions only when nausea subsides
Do NOT consume dairy products — hard to digest when nauseous
Do NOT drink alcohol — worsens stomach irritation and dehydration
Do NOT eat spicy, fried, or greasy foods — they trigger more nausea
Do NOT lie completely flat — keep head elevated at 30 degrees minimum
Do NOT drink fluids too quickly — sip slowly, large gulps trigger vomiting
Do NOT take aspirin or ibuprofen — they irritate stomach; use paracetamol
Do NOT brush teeth immediately after vomiting — stomach acid damages enamel; rinse with water first
Do NOT ignore inability to keep fluids down for more than 12 hours
Do NOT force yourself to eat — wait until nausea passes
home_remedies: Ginger-lemon tea: Grate fresh ginger, add lemon juice and honey to warm water
Peppermint inhalation: Put peppermint oil on a tissue and inhale deeply
Rice water: Boil rice with extra water, strain, sip the starchy liquid — very soothing
Cumin-lemon drink: Roast cumin, grind, mix with lemon juice and water
Clove: Chew on 1-2 cloves — reduces nausea and numbs the gag reflex
Fennel seeds: Chew slowly — calms the stomach
dietary_guidelines: Eat: dry crackers, plain toast, plain rice, bananas, boiled potatoes, clear broth, applesauce
Drink: ORS, water (sipped slowly), ginger tea, peppermint tea, coconut water, clear broth
Avoid: dairy, fried foods, spicy foods, citrus, caffeine, alcohol, raw vegetables, strong-smelling foods
warning_signs: Vomiting blood or material like coffee grounds
Unable to keep any fluids down for 12-24 hours
Signs of severe dehydration: dark urine, dizziness, dry mouth, rapid heartbeat
Severe abdominal pain accompanying vomiting
Vomiting after a head injury
High fever with persistent vomiting
Vomiting with severe headache and stiff neck

description: Pain, scratchiness, or irritation of the throat that worsens when swallowing. 80% caused by viruses, 15-20% by bacteria (strep throat).
key_questions: When did the sore throat start?
Is the pain on one side or both sides?
Does it hurt more when you swallow food, liquids, or saliva?
Can you see any white spots or pus on your tonsils?
Do you have swollen glands in your neck?
Do you have a fever? How high?
Do you have a cough, runny nose, or sneezing along with it?
How much cold water or iced drinks do you consume?
Do you spend a lot of time in air-conditioned rooms?
Do you smoke, vape, or are around smokers?
Have you been talking/singing a lot recently?
Has anyone around you been sick with strep throat?
dos: Gargle with warm salt water (1/2 tsp salt in 1 cup warm water) 4-5 times daily
Drink warm liquids frequently: honey-lemon tea, warm water, tulsi tea, clear broths
Suck on throat lozenges or hard candy to keep throat moist
Use a humidifier to add moisture to dry indoor air
Rest your voice — speak softly and avoid shouting or whispering
Stay well-hydrated — sip fluids throughout the day even if swallowing hurts
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain relief
Eat soft, soothing foods: soups, mashed potatoes, yogurt, smoothies, khichdi, dal
Take 1-2 teaspoons of raw honey — natural antibacterial and soothing properties
Drink warm turmeric milk before bed — anti-inflammatory and promotes healing
Try a saltwater + turmeric gargle — combines antiseptic and anti-inflammatory action
Steam inhalation with eucalyptus oil — soothes throat and clears congestion
donts: Do NOT smoke or vape — smoke severely irritates inflamed throat tissue
Do NOT drink very cold or icy beverages — they worsen irritation
Do NOT shout, sing, or whisper — all three strain the vocal cords
Do NOT eat rough or scratchy foods: chips, crackers, dry toast, nuts
Do NOT eat very spicy or acidic foods — they irritate the throat
Do NOT breathe through your mouth — use saline drops to clear nose
Do NOT take antibiotics unless prescribed by a doctor — most sore throats are viral
Do NOT ignore sore throat with high fever and swollen glands for more than 3 days
Do NOT share utensils, cups, or towels — sore throats are contagious
Do NOT drink alcohol — it dehydrates the throat lining
home_remedies: Mulethi (licorice) tea: Boil mulethi root in water for 10 min — soothes throat inflammation
Honey-turmeric paste: Mix 1 tsp honey with 1/4 tsp turmeric, take before bed
Warm salt-turmeric gargle: Add 1/2 tsp each of salt and turmeric to warm water
Ginger-basil tea: Boil ginger slices and tulsi leaves for 5 min, add honey
Cinnamon tea: Add a cinnamon stick to boiling water — antimicrobial properties
Apple cider vinegar gargle: 1 tbsp ACV in warm water — antibacterial
dietary_guidelines: Eat: soups, broths, mashed potatoes, yogurt, smoothies, warm oatmeal, ice cream/popsicles (for numbing)
Drink: warm honey-lemon water, herbal teas, warm water, chamomile tea, warm milk with turmeric
Avoid: spicy food, acidic fruits (orange, tomato), rough-textured foods, very hot liquids, alcohol, cold drinks
warning_signs: Sore throat lasting more than 7 days
Difficulty swallowing or breathing
High fever (above 38.3C / 101F) with sore throat
Swollen, tender lymph nodes in the neck
White patches or pus on tonsils (possible strep)
Rash accompanying sore throat
Blood in saliva or phlegm
Drooling or inability to swallow saliva (possible abscess)
